title: Genesis 28
subtitle: Jacob's Ladder: The Dream at Bethel

timeline_link.text: See on Timeline — Jacob's Dream at Bethel

sections.header: Verses 1–9 — Isaac Sends Jacob to Paddan Aram

poi.name: Paddan Aram
poi.text: Isaac sends Jacob to Paddan Aram ("the plain of Aram") — the family's Mesopotamian homeland. The journey begins the longest geographical displacement in the patriarchal narrative: Jacob will be away from Canaan for twenty years.

notes.note: MacArthur: Isaac deliberately blesses Jacob with the 'blessing of Abraham' and sends him to find a wife from Laban's daughters. The irony is complete: Isaac's blessing of Jacob at the end of ch.27 was accidental; the blessing he gives at the beginning of ch.28 is intentional. The covenant succession is now formally and consciously passed.

MacArthur: Esau, observing that Canaanite wives displeased his father, took an additional wife from Ishmael's daughters. The attempt to please Isaac by taking a wife from the Ishmaelite line rather than the Canaanite line reveals Esau's continued misunderstanding of what his parents valued. He is trying to correct his mistake with another marriage rather than with covenant character.

heb.paragraph: Isaac's instructions to Jacob — "go at once to Paddan Aram... and take a wife from there" (vv.2-4) — frame the journey as both a covenant endogamy requirement and a covenant transmission. The phrase "God Almighty bless you (wayyĕbārek ʾōtĕkā ʾēl šadday)... the blessing given to Abraham" (v.4) is the formal covenant transmission from Isaac to Jacob: the ʾEl Šadday covenant name and the Abrahamic inheritance are now explicitly passed to Jacob. The flight from Esau is reframed as a covenant commissioning.

Isaac's blessing quotes the Abrahamic covenant formula: "the land God gave to Abraham" (v.4) — "that you may inherit the land where you now reside as a foreigner (megorîm), the land God gave to Abraham." Jacob is being sent away from the land precisely at the moment the covenant transmission promises him that land. The departure from the covenant land is thus not covenant abandonment but covenant exile — the pattern of exile-and-return that will structure all of Israel's subsequent history.

Esau's response to Isaac's instruction (vv.6-9) — marrying a daughter of Ishmael "in addition to the wives he already had" — is a misreading of what his father wanted. He hears the rejection of Canaanite women but misses the point: his additional marriage does not undo the Canaanite marriages already made. The pathos of the detail is that Esau is trying, belatedly, to earn his father's approval through a strategy that cannot work. The covenant blessing cannot be reconstructed through right action after it has been lost through despising covenant privilege.

hist.context: Jacob is at the lowest point of his life — fleeing his brother's murder threat, separated from his family for what will be twenty years, sleeping in the open with a stone for a pillow. It is precisely here that God appears. The Bethel vision is not a reward for virtue but an act of sheer grace: a terrified deceiver encounters the God of Abraham with a personal, direct promise of accompaniment. "I will not leave you" is the covenant's most intimate expression.

refs.note: Jesus explicitly applies the Bethel vision to himself: "You will see heaven opened and the angels of God ascending and descending on the Son of Man." Jacob's ladder is the type; Christ is the antitype — the true stairway between heaven and earth.
The "I am the LORD your God" formula at Sinai echoes Bethel. The Sinai covenant expands what Bethel establishes: God's personal presence and protection.

"I am with you always, to the end of the age" — Jesus' Great Commission promise echoes "I am with you and will keep you wherever you go." The Bethel promise is fulfilled in the risen Christ's abiding presence.

sarna.source: Nahum Sarna, JPS Torah Commentary — Scholarly Paraphrase

notes.note: Isaac sends Jacob to Paddan Aram with an explicit covenant commission: "May God Almighty bless you and make you fruitful and increase your numbers... May he give you and your descendants the blessing given to Abraham." Sarna: "The formal sending of Jacob with the Abrahamic blessing is the transition point: Jacob leaves Canaan as the bearer of the covenant. The departure is simultaneously a flight from Esau and a divinely commissioned journey toward the fulfilment of the promise."

Esau observes that the daughters of Canaan displeased Isaac and goes to marry Ishmael's daughter. Sarna: "Esau's action is pathetically belated and theologically revealing: he tries to win his parents' approval by observing a rule he didn't know before. The action shows his fundamental incapacity to grasp what the covenant requires. Marrying Ishmael's daughter brings him closer to the covenant family but further from its spirit."

alter.source: Robert Alter, The Hebrew Bible: A Translation with Commentary — Scholarly Paraphrase

notes.note: Esau goes to add Ishmael's daughter to his wives. Alter: "The episode is told with characteristic brevity — three verses, no dialogue, no emotional response. But the poignancy is immense: Esau, who lost the birthright by not caring about it and lost the blessing by being deceived, now tries to compensate for his failures by marrying the right person. It is the action of a man who can see what he should have done but cannot understand why he failed."

Esau finally realises after all the deceptions that Canaanite wives displease his father. Alter notes Esau's response is to add yet another wife rather than to change himself. The person who learns the lesson too late and applies it inadequately is a recurring type in Genesis.

Isaac blesses Jacob with the Abrahamic blessing consciously this time. Calvin: "Having before been deceived, Isaac now, in his right mind and with clear intention, confers the same blessing on Jacob. He makes no attempt to revoke the first blessing; rather, he confirms it. This shows that Isaac, whatever his initial confusion, ultimately yields to the divine will."

Calvin: Now Isaac blesses Jacob knowingly — the same blessing given unknowingly in ch.27 is confirmed deliberately. God used the deception to achieve what Isaac's intention had blocked; now Isaac's intention catches up with God's purpose.

net.source: NET Bible Full Notes Edition — Biblical Studies Press

notes.note: The NET note on El Shaddai (God Almighty) in Isaac's blessing connects to 17:1, where the same divine name was used in the covenant renewal with Abraham. The note observes the deliberate echo: the covenant that began with El Shaddai appearing to Abraham is now transmitted to Jacob under the same divine name.

The NET note on the Abrahamic blessing formula transmitted to Jacob (land, seed, descendants) observes Isaac uses the language of ch.17's covenant renewal — this is the most precise covenant language Isaac ever speaks.

sections.header: Verses 10–22 — Jacob's Dream at Bethel; the Vow

poi.name: Bethel
poi.text: Jacob stops for the night at a "certain place" — unnamed until after the dream, when it receives the name Bethel (house of God). The unremarkable stopping point becomes the most theologically charged location in the Jacob story. The gate of heaven is not a shrine but a sleeping place that the divine chose.

notes.note: MacArthur: 'Surely the LORD is in this place, and I was not aware of it' -- Jacob's response to the Bethel vision is awe at the unexpectedness of the divine presence. The holy dread (v.17: 'how awesome is this place') is the appropriate response to the realisation that the covenant God is present in an ordinary place, not only in designated sanctuaries.

MacArthur: Jacob's vow is conditional -- 'if God will be with me... then the LORD will be my God.' The conditionality is not unbelief but the beginning of covenant faith. The NT equivalent would be the young believer who is learning what it means to trust. God does not reject conditional faith; he works with it, as the twenty years of Jacob's life in Haran will demonstrate.

heb.paragraph: Jacob's dream at Bethel — "a stairway (sullām) resting on the earth, with its top reaching to heaven, and the angels of God were ascending and descending on it" (v.12) — is one of Scripture's great theophanic visions. The sullām (from the root sālal — to pile up, to build a raised road) is an architectural image: a ramp connecting earth to heaven, with divine messengers in constant transit. Jesus applies this image to himself in John 1:51: "you will see heaven open, and the angels of God ascending and descending on the Son of Man." Jacob's ladder is a Christological type.

heb.gloss: the LORD, the God of Abraham
heb.paragraph: The divine self-identification — "I am the LORD, the God of your father Abraham and the God of Isaac" (v.13) — is the covenant name formula that will become the standard self-identification throughout Exodus and the prophets. The genealogical concatenation (God of Abraham and Isaac) is now extended to include Jacob: "I will give you and your descendants the land on which you are lying" (v.13). The promise is given to the fleeing, homeless, exiled Jacob — the covenant God pursues his own into their lowest moments.

Jacob's vow (neder, vv.20-22) — "if God will be with me and will watch over me... then the LORD will be my God" — is the first vow in Scripture and has puzzled interpreters: is Jacob bargaining with God? But the Hebrew conditional (ʾim — "if") can also express a temporal sense ("when"), and the vow may be better read as a declaration of future covenant commitment contingent on the fulfilment of what God has just promised. Jacob is not negotiating but responding to divine promise with a conditional promise of his own — faith exercised in covenant terms.

hist.context: Jacob's response to the vision has three movements: fear ("How awesome is this place"), naming (Bethel), and consecration (the stone pillar). The sequence is the prototype of all later sanctuary construction — encountering the holy leads to marking the place and establishing a worship site. The stone Jacob used as a pillow becomes an altar. The ordinary becomes sacred by divine encounter.

refs.note: Jesus tells Nathanael, 'You will see heaven open, and the angels of God ascending and descending on the Son of Man.' Jacob's ladder becomes a christological image: Jesus is the stairway connecting heaven and earth.
God later commands Jacob to return to Bethel and build an altar. The vow made here (28:20–22) is fulfilled there — the narrative arc spans seven chapters and twenty years.
Hebrews includes Jacob among the heroes of faith. The Bethel encounter — where Jacob receives the promise despite his character — demonstrates that faith rests on God's initiative, not human merit.

sarna.source: Nahum Sarna, JPS Torah Commentary — Scholarly Paraphrase

notes.note: The Bethel dream is the most important divine communication to Jacob in his entire narrative. Sarna: "The ladder (or ramp) connecting heaven and earth with angels ascending and descending represents the continuous divine-human traffic of prayer and providence. The YHWH who stands above the ladder and speaks to Jacob is the same God of Abraham and Isaac — the covenant is personal, not merely institutional."

Jacob awakes and declares: "Surely the LORD is in this place, and I was not aware of it." Sarna: "The statement is theologically programmatic: the covenant God is present at unexpected places and times. The holiness of Bethel is not its own inherent quality but the divine presence that has manifested there. Jacob's fear — 'how awesome is this place!' — is the appropriate creaturely response to the presence of the holy."

alter.source: Robert Alter, The Hebrew Bible: A Translation with Commentary — Scholarly Paraphrase

notes.note: The ladder (sullam) with angels ascending and descending. Alter: "The image is one of the most visually memorable in the Bible — a bridge between earth and heaven, thronging with divine messengers. Alter notes that the angels ascend first and then descend, suggesting they are earth-based angels going up to receive instructions and returning — or simply that the vision presents continuous two-way divine-human communication."

Jacob's vow — "if God will be with me... then the LORD will be my God" — is conditional in a way that Abraham's responses to God were not. Alter: "Jacob bargains with God as he has bargained with everyone else. The conditionality of his vow — 'if' — is a characterological detail. He is the man of negotiation even in the presence of the divine. Yet the vow is genuine: he will build a house of God, and he will give a tenth."

"Surely the LORD is in this place, and I was not aware of it." Calvin: "God reveals his presence in places and ways we do not expect. Jacob had fled from his father's house in fear and shame; yet the LORD meets him on the road. This teaches us that the presence of God is not confined to holy places but follows his people wherever they go." The ubiquity of the divine presence against the theology of sacred geography.

"This is none other than the house of God; this is the gate of heaven." Calvin: "Jacob calls Bethel the house of God and the gate of heaven — the place of divine access. This is the beginning of the sanctuary theology that will culminate in the tabernacle and temple. The place where God reveals himself becomes the place from which humans approach God."

net.source: NET Bible Full Notes Edition — Biblical Studies Press

notes.note: The NET note on sullam (ladder/stairway) discusses the etymology and parallel with Mesopotamian ziggurats, which were called "stairways to heaven." The note notes John 1:51 — Jesus saying "you will see heaven open, and the angels of God ascending and descending on the Son of Man" — which reads Jacob's ladder as a type of Christ himself as the mediator between heaven and earth.

The NET note on Jacob's vow to give a tenth discusses whether this constitutes the origin of tithing or simply reflects a pre-existing practice. The note connects to Abraham's tithe to Melchizedek (14:20) and the later Mosaic tithe law. The note observes that Jacob's vow is conditional — if God keeps his promises — which makes it more of a responsive pledge than an unconditional act of worship.

ppl.name: Jacob
ppl.role: Fleeing deceiver; recipient of unmerited grace
ppl.text: At his morally lowest point, he receives God's most intimate promise. This is the pattern of grace throughout Scripture — it meets people in flight, not in arrival. The Bethel encounter will define Jacob's identity for the rest of his life.

chapter_panels.trans: VersionTranslationNIV"He had a dream in which he saw a stairway resting on the earth, with its top reaching to heaven, and the angels of God were ascending and descending on it."ESV"And he dreamed, and behold, there was a ladder set up on the earth, and the top of it reached to heaven. And behold, the angels of God were ascending and descending on it!"KJV"And he dreamed, and behold a ladder set up on the earth, and the top of it reached to heaven: and behold the angels of God ascending and descending on it."NASB"He had a dream, and behold, a ladder was set up on the earth with its top reaching to heaven; and behold, the angels of God were ascending and descending on it."

src.title: Mesopotamian Ziggurat Architecture
src.quote: The Babylonian ziggurat ("mountain of God") featured a monumental staircase connecting earth to the temple at the summit. The Sumerian word simmiltu (staircase/ramp) closely resembles Hebrew sullām. Jacob's vision may deliberately reframe the Babel/ziggurat imagery: not human ascent to the divine but divine descent to the human.

src.title: Dead Sea Scrolls 4Q537
src.quote: A fragmentary Aramaic text from Qumran describes Jacob's Bethel vision with additional angelic detail, showing the passage was extensively meditated upon in Second Temple Judaism.

rec.text: Jesus' direct application: "You will see heaven opened, and the angels of God ascending and descending on the Son of Man." The Bethel ladder is explicitly fulfilled in Christ. This is the most direct NT Christological reading of any Genesis passage apart from Genesis 22.

Augustine read the Bethel stone as a type of Christ — the cornerstone rejected by builders — and the anointing oil as a type of Christ's anointing (Christos = "anointed one"). The stone becomes the foundation of the house of God.

rows.label: Bethel: The Gate of Heaven
rows.text: Surely the LORD is in this place, and I was not aware of it. The divine presence does not announce itself; it is recognised in retrospect. Bethel (house of God) is named for the ordinary sleeping-place of a fugitive.

rows.label: The Conditional Vow: Faith at Its Beginning
rows.text: If God will be with me... then the LORD will be my God. The conditional form is honest: Jacob is at the beginning of his faith journey. The vow is not unbelief but the faith that has not yet grown beyond conditions.

hebtext.note: Isaac's instructions to Jacob — "go at once to Paddan Aram... and take a wife from there" (vv.2-4) — frame the journey as both a covenant endogamy requirement and a covenant transmission. The phrase "God Almighty bless you (wayyĕbārek ʾōtĕkā ʾēl šadday)... the blessing given to Abraham" (v.4) is the formal covenant transmission from Isaac to Jacob: the ʾEl Šadday covenant name and the Abrahamic inheritance are now explicitly passed to Jacob. The flight from Esau is reframed as a covenant commissioning.

Isaac's blessing quotes the Abrahamic covenant formula: "the land God gave to Abraham" (v.4) — "that you may inherit the land where you now reside as a foreigner (megorîm), the land God gave to Abraham." Jacob is being sent away from the land precisely at the moment the covenant transmission promises him that land. The departure from the covenant land is thus not covenant abandonment but covenant exile — the pattern of exile-and-return that will structure all of Israel's subsequent history.

Esau's response to Isaac's instruction (vv.6-9) — marrying a daughter of Ishmael "in addition to the wives he already had" — is a misreading of what his father wanted. He hears the rejection of Canaanite women but misses the point: his additional marriage does not undo the Canaanite marriages already made. The pathos of the detail is that Esau is trying, belatedly, to earn his father's approval through a strategy that cannot work. The covenant blessing cannot be reconstructed through right action after it has been lost through despising covenant privilege.

Jacob's dream at Bethel — "a stairway (sullām) resting on the earth, with its top reaching to heaven, and the angels of God were ascending and descending on it" (v.12) — is one of Scripture's great theophanic visions. The sullām (from the root sālal — to pile up, to build a raised road) is an architectural image: a ramp connecting earth to heaven, with divine messengers in constant transit. Jesus applies this image to himself in John 1:51: "you will see heaven open, and the angels of God ascending and descending on the Son of Man." Jacob's ladder is a Christological type.

hebtext.gloss: the LORD, the God of Abraham
hebtext.note: The divine self-identification — "I am the LORD, the God of your father Abraham and the God of Isaac" (v.13) — is the covenant name formula that will become the standard self-identification throughout Exodus and the prophets. The genealogical concatenation (God of Abraham and Isaac) is now extended to include Jacob: "I will give you and your descendants the land on which you are lying" (v.13). The promise is given to the fleeing, homeless, exiled Jacob — the covenant God pursues his own into their lowest moments.

Jacob's vow (neder, vv.20-22) — "if God will be with me and will watch over me... then the LORD will be my God" — is the first vow in Scripture and has puzzled interpreters: is Jacob bargaining with God? But the Hebrew conditional (ʾim — "if") can also express a temporal sense ("when"), and the vow may be better read as a declaration of future covenant commitment contingent on the fulfilment of what God has just promised. Jacob is not negotiating but responding to divine promise with a conditional promise of his own — faith exercised in covenant terms.

tx.title: Jacob's ladder — sullām
tx.content: MT's sullām ('ladder/stairway') occurs only here in the entire Hebrew Bible. LXX translates as κλῖμαξ ('ladder/staircase'). The Vulgate uses 'scala.' The hapax legomenon makes the exact referent uncertain — it could be a ramp, stairway, or ziggurat ramp. Targum Onqelos retains the Hebrew word untranslated.
tx.note: The Mesopotamian ziggurat parallel (a ramp connecting heaven and earth) is widely noted. Onqelos's decision not to translate may reflect uncertainty about the word's meaning even in antiquity.

tx.title: Bethel naming — SP and LXX agreement
tx.content: MT records the naming of Bethel, noting the city was formerly called Luz. The SP and LXX agree with MT here. However, the SP's broader program inserts Gerizim references into the Pentateuch, and its treatment of Bethel — a rival sanctuary to both Jerusalem and Gerizim — is notably restrained in this passage.
tx.note: The SP's restraint at Bethel is surprising given its Gerizim theology. It may reflect the passage's antiquity or the difficulty of plausibly altering such a well-known tradition.

debate.title: Jacob's Vow (28:20-22): Conditional Faith or Genuine Commitment?

positions.name: Conditional/bargaining
positions.argument: "If God does these things for me, then the LORD will be my God." The conditional structure (im... vehayyah YHWH li le'Elohim) suggests Jacob is negotiating rather than committing — a commercially minded response to a divine gift.

positions.name: Authentic response to grace
positions.argument: The vow follows the covenant promise (vv.13-15); it is a response to grace, not a precondition for it. The "if" clause restates the promise in Jacob's own words. The vow is the form in which ancient people expressed grateful commitment.
